coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent nanoplatform for all-in-one sensing and removal of acrolein: An ultrasensitive probe to evaluate its removal efficiency

coredata.dc:description: As a highly toxic aldehyde, acrolein is widely found in diet and environment, and can be produced endogenously, posing a serious threat to human health. Herein, we designed a novel fluorescent nanoplatform integrating carbon dots‑manganese dioxide (CDs-MnO2) and glutathione (GSH) for all-in-one sensing and removal of acrolein. By converting Mn4+ to free Mn2+, GSH inhibited the inner filter effect (IFE) of MnO2 nanosheets, and the Michael addition of acrolein with GSH inhibited the GSH-induced Mn4+ conversion, forming an “off-on-off” fluorescence response of CDs. The developed fluorescent nanoplatform exhibited high sensitivity (LOD was 0.067 μM) and selectivity for the simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein. The combination of CDs-MnO2 hydrogels with smartphones realized the point-of-care detection of acrolein, yielding satisfactory results (recovery rates varied between 97.01–104.65%, and RSD ranged from 1.42 to 4.16%). Moreover, the capability of the nanoplatform was investigated for on-site evaluating acrolein scavengers' efficacy, demonstrating excellent potential for practical application.

(c) XRD patterns of MnO2 NSs and CDs-MnO2. (d) Zate potentials of CDs, MnO2 NSs, and CDs-MnO2. (e) XPS spectrum of CDs-MnO2 in the wide scan survey. (f-i) High-resolution peaks of XPS survey of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Mn 2p. (j-l) TEM images of CDs, MnO2 NSs, and CDs-MnO2. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a,b) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 (black line), CDs-MnO2 with GSH (100.0 μM) (red line), CDs-MnO2 with GSH (100.0 μM)/acrolein (100.0 μM) (blue line). λex = 384 nm, λem=442 nm. (c,d) UV–vis absorption of CDs-MnO2 (10.0 μM) upon addition of the different concentrations of GSH (0–100.0 μM) and GSH (100.0 μM)/acrolein (0–100.0 μM), respectively. (e,f) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 solution (10.0 μM) upon addition of the different concentrations of GSH (0–100.0 μM) and GSH (100.0 μM)/acrolein (0–100.0 μM), respectively. (g,h) The linear relationships of CDs-MnO2 fluorescent intensity versus the concentration of GSH (0–30.0 μM) and acrolein (0–40.0 μM), respectively. λex = 384 nm, λem=442 nm.

(a) Scheme of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel for the detection of GSH and acrolein. (b) Fluorescence photos of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel after reacting with different concentrations of GSH solution under a 365 nm UV light (From left to right, 0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100.0 μM). Fluorescence photos of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel after reacting with a mixture of acrolein at different concentrations (From left to right, 0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 60.0, 80.0, 100.0 μM) and 100.0 μM GSH under a 365 nm UV light. (c) Schematic diagram of CDs-MnO2 hydrogel sensing platform combined with a smartphone to detect acrolein. The linear relationship between R + G and concentrations of acrolein (0–20.0 μM).

(a) UV–vis absorption of acrolein (100.0 μM) upon addition of the different concentrations of GSH (0–100.0 μM). (b) The linear relationship between absorbance of acrolein at 315 nm and concentrations of GSH (0–60.0 μM). (c) The free acrolein was measured at different time intervals after incubation with GSH scavengers by CDs-MnO2 hydrogel and UV spectrophotometry method respectively. (d-f) The free acrolein was measured at different time intervals after incubation with three scavengers (curcumin, hesperetin, and resveratrol).

(a) Schematic illustration of the working principle of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel for point-of-care detection of acrolein released from (b) baijiu, (c) deep-fried meat skewers and (d) burning cigarette was monitored by CDs-MnO2 hydrogel under daylight and UV light.

Fluorescence imaging acrolein in GL261 cells. (a) GL261 cells were cultured with CDs-MnO2 (10.0 μM) for 12 h; (b) GL261 cells were cultured with LA (100.0 μM) for 2 h, and then incubated with CDs-MnO2 (10.0 μM) for 12 h; (c) GL261 cells were cultured with with LA (100.0 μM) for 2 h, and then incubated with acrolein (100.0 μM) for 2 h, and then incubated with CDs-MnO2 (10.0 μM) for 12 h. The scale bars are 100 μm. λex = 359 nm.

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent nanoplatform for all-in-one sensing and removal of acrolein: An ultrasensitive probe to evaluate its removal efficiency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a highly toxic aldehyde, acrolein is widely found in diet and environment, and can be produced endogenously, posing a serious threat to human health. Herein, we designed a novel fluorescent nanoplatform integrating carbon dots‑manganese dioxide (CDs-MnO2) and glutathione (GSH) for all-in-one sensing and removal of acrolein. By converting Mn4+ to free Mn2+, GSH inhibited the inner filter effect (IFE) of MnO2 nanosheets, and the Michael addition of acrolein with GSH inhibited the GSH-induced Mn4+ conversion, forming an “off-on-off” fluorescence response of CDs. The developed fluorescent nanoplatform exhibited high sensitivity (LOD was 0.067 μM) and selectivity for the simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein. The combination of CDs-MnO2 hydrogels with smartphones realized the point-of-care detection of acrolein, yielding satisfactory results (recovery rates varied between 97.01–104.65%, and RSD ranged from 1.42 to 4.16%). Moreover, the capability of the nanoplatform was investigated for on-site evaluating acrolein scavengers' efficacy, demonstrating excellent potential for practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The identification and removal of toxic pollutants is of significant importance for sustainable development. However, achieving integration of identification and removal on a single platform remains a major challenge in the scientific community. Acrolein (2-propenal), a highly reactive α,β-unsaturated aldehyde, serves as a crucial synthetic intermediate widely used in various industrial production fields including polymer materials, coating materials, pharmaceuticals, and insecticides (Estevez et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2012). Simultaneously, acrolein is a prevalent environmental pollutant posing severe threats to human health and life. Research has found that acrolein can be detected in food, the environment, and the human body (Feng et al., 2006; Stevens & Maier, 2008; Zou et al., 2021). In the human body, acrolein readily reacts with biomolecules such as proteins, DNA, and nucleic acids, leading to toxic effects, resulting in protein dysfunction, enzyme inactivation, and genetic damage (Moghe et al., 2015). Acrolein can also indirectly induce toxicity through pathways such as oxidative stress induction, mitochondrial dysfunction, and endoplasmic reticulum stress (Aizenbud et al., 2016; Esterbauer et al., 1991; Seaman et al., 2006). Many studies have linked acrolein to neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, multiple sclerosis, strokes, and spinal cord injuries (Ambaw et al., 2018; Dang et al., 2010; Luo & Shi, 2005). Acrolein also plays a crucial role in the progression of diseases such as atherosclerosis, cardiovascular diseases, and kidney diseases (Igarashi et al., 2018; Saiki et al., 2009). Precisely because of the high toxicity and widespread presence of acrolein, considerable efforts are being made to develop new methods for effectively detecting and removing acrolein. For example, fluorescence analysis based on the Skraup reaction with m-aminophenol (Alarcon, 1968; Yale & Bernstein, 1948) and various small-molecule fluorescent sensors are used for selective and sensitive detection of acrolein. On the other hand, various scavengers such as curcumin (Jiang et al., 2021), hesperetin (Wang et al., 2015), and resveratrol (Zhang et al., 2022) have been developed for acrolein removal, showing promising removal efficiency. However, most of currently developed methods can only accomplish either detection or removal tasks independently, limiting their practical applications. Therefore, achieving the simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein, which poses threats to environmental safety and human health, remains a challenge.

Currently, the detection methods for acrolein mainly include acrolein derivatization followed by LC/GC–MS (Bohnenstengel et al., 1997; Custodio-Mendoza et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2005), isotope dilution (Ewert et al., 2011), the 3-hydroxypropyl mercapturic acid technique based on acrolein metabolites (Tully et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2010), and the immunoblotting method utilizing acrolein antibodies (Uchida et al., 1998). Although these methods exhibit high sensitivity, their sample pretreatment processes and operational procedures are relatively cumbersome, often relying on bulky instruments, lacking convenience and immediacy, thus not suitable for on-site and point-of-care detection or analysis of biological samples. In recent years, fluorescence detection has gradually become a focal point in the field of acrolein detection due to its convenience and rapidity. Several small-molecule fluorescent probes have emerged for detecting acrolein in food and cells (Jiang et al., 2022; Pradipta et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2023; Togashi et al., 2010; Togashi et al., 2014; Xu, Zhang, Huai, et al., 2022; Xu, Zhang, Zhang, et al., 2022). To provide a more intuitive comparison, we summarized these probes reported in the current literature in Table 1 . Although these fluorescent probes can detect acrolein, they do not demonstrate the convenience of point-of-care detection. Evaluation of removal efficiency still requires the assistance of instruments, limiting on-site evaluation of the removal process and its efficacy. Moreover, for the removal of acrolein, adding scavengers is the most effective way to remove the already generated acrolein (Qiu et al., 2022). Commonly used acrolein scavengers mainly include antioxidants, nitrogen-containing compounds, and sulfur-containing compounds (Jiang et al., 2020; Uemura et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2024). Research has found that, as an important endogenous antioxidant in cells, glutathione (GSH) can undergo a Michael addition reaction with acrolein. Binding with GSH and being excreted in urine as a mercapturic acid metabolite is the main detoxification pathway of acrolein in the body (Aldini et al., 2011; Gupta et al., 2021). However, the single function of individual sensing or removal system limits their further application. Conversely, integrated detection and removal technologies, especially those combining fluorescence detection with removal, have received widespread attention. However, traditional fluorescent probes or scavengers can only achieve a single function and it is challenging to achieve simultaneous detection and removal. Therefore, developing a novel sensing platform with all-in-one sensing and removal functions is an urgent need for efficient detection and removal of acrolein in food, environment, and living cells.

As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have high water solubility, excellent optical properties, good chemical stability, excellent biological compatibility, and low toxicity, attracting widespread attention in the fields of fluorescence sensing and bioimaging (Lin et al., 2024; Xia et al., 2019). Additionally, manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) represent a novel layered two-dimensional nanostructure characterized by low toxicity, broad UV–vis absorption spectra, high surface area, low cost, and excellent environmental compatibility (Sohal et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). MnO2 NSs exhibit a broad absorption spectrum that effectively overlaps with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of most fluorescent materials, enabling fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) between MnO2 NSs and fluorescent substances, thereby endowing MnO2 NSs with excellent fluorescence quenching capabilities (Gao et al., 2024; Peng et al., 2019). Furthermore, MnO2 NSs possess robust peroxidase-like activity. In the presence of reducing agents such as GSH, MnO2 NSs can be decomposed into Mn2+ ions, leading to fluorescence recovery (Hiremath et al., 2022). Consequently, MnO2 NSs have found widespread applications in the detection of reducing substances. Based on this strategy, a sensing platform assembled with CDs-MnO2 has been employed for the selective detection of a series of analytes including GSH (Wang et al., 2022), organophosphorus compounds (Yan et al., 2018), uric acid (Amjadi et al., 2018), ascorbic acid (Wang et al., 2019), and biological thiols (Garg et al., 2018).

Inspired by the aforementioned studies, our study innovatively designs a novel fluorescent nanoplatform composed of CDs-MnO2 and GSH. As a multifunctional system, this nanoplatform can simultaneously achieve sensitive detection and effective removal of acrolein (Scheme 1 ). MnO2 quenched the fluorescence of CDs through IFE. GSH could reduce MnO2 NSs to Mn2+, leading to the release of CDs and fluorescence recovery. In the presence of acrolein, GSH first reacted with acrolein through Michael addition, resulting in acrolein removal. Simultaneously, the formation of GSH-acrolein adducts blocked the reduction of MnO2 by GSH, transforming the fluorescence response mode from the original “off-on” pattern to an “on-off” pattern (Scheme 1a). This change in fluorescence mode can be utilized for highly sensitive detection of acrolein. By combining CDs-MnO2 with GSH, the removal efficiency of acrolein was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of samples with different concentrations of acrolein. In contrast to previous strategies that introduced a thiol group into the molecular structure of fluorescent probes, our designed nanoplatform directly utilized the specific binding of acrolein with the thiol-containing reducing agent GSH to inhibit the reduction and decomposition of CDs-MnO2 by GSH. To improve the convenience of practical application, we prepared CDs-MnO2 hydrogels and combined them with a smartphone to achieve portable and point-of-care detection of acrolein in food samples and ambient air of living environments (Scheme 1b), yielding satisfactory results. Furthermore, we investigated the ability of the nanoplatform for on-site evaluating the efficacy of acrolein scavengers. Additionally, this nanoplatform was successfully applied for the detection of acrolein in living cells (Scheme 1c). The fluorescent nanoplatform designed in this study is the first to combine CDs-MnO2 for detecting acrolein, enabling integration of acrolein detection and removal. This paves a bright path for all-in-one point-of-care sensing and efficient removal of acrolein in food and the environment, also providing an important reference strategy for the diagnosis and treatment of acrolein-related diseases in clinical settings.

Reduced glutathione (GSH), tetrahydrate manganese chloride (MnCl2·4H2O), tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMA·OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), citric acid, ethylenediamine, glucose, glutamate, glycine, histidine, proline, leucine, and α-lipoic acid (LA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade, and used directly without further purification. All water used was ultrapure water produced by a Millipore Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, USA) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.

The morphology and composition of the prepared materials were characterized using XRD (AXS-D8, Bruker, Germany), TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company, USA), XPS (Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi), and FTIR (Shimadzu FTIR-8400 s, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectroscopy in the range of 200–800 nm was recorded on a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The samples were excited at 384 nm, and the fluorescence emission ranged from 300 to 600 nm with a step of 1 nm. All UV–vis absorption and fluorescence measurements were carried out at room temperature. pH values were measured using a pH meter (INESA Scientific Inc., China). Cell imaging was performed using an FV3000 laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan). In this study, all the fluorescence images were captured in a closed black box with a 365 nm UV analyzer (ZF-2, Anting, China).

According to the literature (Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022), MnO2 NSs were prepared with slight modifications. 10 mL MnCl2·4H2O (0.3 M) and 20 mL TMA•OH (0.6 M) (containing 30 wt% H2O2) were immediately mixed in a 100 mL round-bottom flask. After vigorous stirring at room temperature for 12 h, the resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to obtain MnO2 NSs. Then, they were washed 5 times with ultrapure water and methanol, respectively. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 50 °C. After dispersion in ultrapure water, the resulting suspension formed a brown solution through ultrasonication.

Based on the process reported in the literature, a small modification was made to synthesize CDs (Amjadi et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2022). 250 mg of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and then 0.5 mL of ethylenediamine was added. After sufficient stirring, the solution was transferred to a reaction kettle with a PTFE inner liner (30 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the product was transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da and continuously dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h to remove unreacted precursor molecules. Finally, the prepared CDs solution was stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) for further use.

Based on the process reported in the literature, a small modification was made to synthesize CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites (Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). 0.04 mL of CDs solution was added to 12 mL of MnO2 solution and ultrasonicated for 30 min to obtain the CDs-MnO2 solution, which was then stored in a refrigerator for further use.

To test the stability of CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, the prepared CDs-MnO2 was storage under a 365 nm UV light for 0 h, 1 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h and 48 h, respectively. Then the fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 for GSH and acrolein response was measured after exposure to UV light. In addition, the prepared CDs-MnO2 was placed in the dark at different temperatures (−10 °C, 4 °C, 25 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, 80 °C, and 100 °C) for 24 h. Then the fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 for GSH and acrolein response was measured after storage at different temperatures. Furthermore, a thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of CDs-MnO2 was performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of GSH recognition by CDs-MnO2
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the ability of GSH to restore the quenched fluorescence of CDs in CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, different concentrations of GSH (0–100.0 μM) were mixed with the CDs-MnO2 solution in a series of colorimetric tubes. After 1 h, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were measured, respectively. To confirm the specific recognition of GSH by CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, selective experiments were conducted to investigate the optical response of CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites to a series of analytes such as GSH, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, PO3 2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, glucose, glutamate, glycine, histidine, proline, and leucine. The interference experiment was performed in the presence of various mental ions included Na+, Ag+, Mg2, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cs2+, Li+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of acrolein recognition by CDs-MnO2
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the GSH solution was mixed with different concentrations of acrolein (0–100.0 μM) for 1 h and then added to the CDs-MnO2 solution in a series of colorimetric tubes. After incubation for 1 h, the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were measured. To verify the selective recognition of acrolein by CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, the selective experiment was conducted to study the optical response of the CDs-MnO2/GSH system to various analytes such as acrolein, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, glyoxal, methylglyoxal, acetone, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, PO3 2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NH3·H2O, and N2H4·H2O. The interference experiment was performed in the presence of various mental ions included Na+, Ag+, Mg2, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cs2+, Li+, Fe3+, Hg2+, and Zn2+.

50 mg of agarose was added to 10 mL water and the solution was heated at 100 °C until it was completely dissolved. Then 1 mL CDs-MnO2 solution was added to the mixture and stirred continuously for 10 min to ensure uniform mixing. The prepared hydrogel was pressed into circular films using a mold and then placed in a glass dish to cool and solidify after demolding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of acrolein by CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films combined with a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, various concentrations of acrolein solution (0–100.0 μM) were added to the GSH solution (100.0 μM). After 15 min, the resulting series of mixed solution was dropped onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films. After 15 min, photos of the hydrogel films were captured using a smartphone under a 365 nm UV light. Then, the images were analyzed using the “Detection” app to obtain RGB values. The precision of the approach was further assessed as intra- and inter-day precisions by analyzing three distinct concentrations on the same day or on three separate days in a row, respectively.

Furthermore, five different brands of mobile phones (Vivo S18, Huawei Mate 20, Horner Magic 6, Oppo Realme RMX 3576, and Redmi note 10) were selected and installed the “Color analyzer” software for further use as testing smartphones. Before taking photos, the camera settings of all five phones were ensured to be identical. Then, these five smartphones were used to take photos of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel, CDs-MnO2 hydrogel+GSH, and CDs-MnO2 hydrogel+GSH/acrolein under a 365 nm UV light. Subsequently, the hydrogel photos captured by each phone were imported into their respective “Color analyzer” software for analysis and then RGB values were exported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein, and on-site evaluation of the scavenging capacity of acrolein scavengers
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we investigated the UV–vis absorption spectra of acrolein (100.0 μM) with different concentrations of GSH (0–100.0 μM). After terminating the reaction by HCl at the final concentration of 1 mM, a portion of the reaction solution was taken and detected for the changes of acrolein at its maximum absorption wavelength of 315 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. To further validate the integrated ability of the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform to detect and remove acrolein, we simultaneously used two methods, the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform with a smartphone method (The details of the detecting method refer to Section 2.8.) and the UV spectroscopy method to detect and remove a certain amount of acrolein. The results of above two methods were compared.

In addition, on-site evaluation of the scavenging capacity of acrolein scavengers by CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform was also investigated. Three acrolein scavenger solutions (curcumin, hesperetin, and resveratrol) were prepared using PBS solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) with a concentration of 5 mM for each of the three scavengers. Take a certain volume of acrolein and add it to the PBS solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) to prepare acrolein stock solution (5 mM). 2 mL acrolein solution (5 mM) was mixed with 2 mL acrolein scavenger solution (5 mM) into glass tubes. After vortex mixing, place the tubes in a dark environment at 37 °C and react for 5, 15, 30, and 60 min. The blank control group uses the same volume of PBS instead of the acrolein scavenger solution. Samples were taken at each time point and immediately subjected to an ice bath, followed by adding 2 μL HCl to terminate the reaction. Then, after adding a certain amount of GSH solution and mixing, a small amount of the above solution was dropped onto CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film. After 5 min, a photo of the hydrogel was taken with a smartphone. Based on the RGB values exported by the smartphone, the linear relationship between RGB values and acrolein concentration was used to calculate the remaining acrolein content. All analyses were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of acrolein in food samples and ambient air of daily living spaces by CDs-MnO2 hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practicality and convenience of CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films, we applied them to detect acrolein in food samples and ambient air in daily living spaces. Baijiu (Chinese liquor) and deep-fried meat skewers were chosen as food samples. Each food sample was placed in a sealed culture dish along with a GSH solution and left for 24 h. At different time points, GSH solution was extracted from the culture dish using a pipette and dropped onto CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films. After 15 min, the hydrogel films were placed under a 365 nm UV light, and photos were taken using a smartphone to read RGB values. We also performed the same method to detect acrolein released from burning cigarettes.

To further validate the accuracy of CDs-MnO2 in recognizing and detecting acrolein, we conducted recovery experiments using CDs-MnO2 hydrogel on various food samples (potato chips, cheese, and apple juice) and ambient air samples from different locations (kitchen, smoking room, and barbecue restaurant). For food samples, 1 g of ground food samples was suspended in 30 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and ultrasonication was performed for 1 h. Then the supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 30 min for acrolein determination. For ambient air samples, GSH solution was placed in the kitchen, smoking room, and barbecue restaurant for 24 h. Then CDs-MnO2 hydrogel was used to detect acrolein in the food supernatant and GSH solution from different locations. Acrolein content in these food samples was calculated based on the standard curve, and recovery experiments for acrolein determination were performed by adding acrolein (1–5 μM) to the food sample solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites was tested using GL261 cells (mouse glioma cells) via the MTT method. Initially, cells were seeded in a 96-well plate containing DMEM medium with 10% FBS, 50 mg·mL−1 penicillin, and 50 mg·mL−1 streptomycin and cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Different concentrations (0–50 μg·mL−1) of CDs-MnO2 were added to each well and further incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, MTT solution (0.5 mg·mL−1) was added and incubated for an additional 4 h. After removing excess MTT solution, DMSO was added and the absorbance at 490 nm was measured using a microplate reader.

GL261 cells were initially seeded in 35 mm cell culture dishes and cultured for 12 h. Before imaging, cells were washed three times with PBS solution (0.01 M, pH 7.4), and all cells were randomly divided into four groups: Control group, Group a, Group b, and Group c. To demonstrate the specificity of GSH in fluorescence recovery, Group b and Group c were pretreated with 500 μM LA for 2 h to promote GSH production and increase its concentration. Group c was treated with 500 μM acrolein after LA pretreatment for 2 h. After 2 h, except for the control group, all groups (a, b, c) were incubated with CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites (200 μg·mL−1) in the culture medium for 12 h. After incubation, cells were washed three times with PBS, fixed with paraformaldehyde, and stained with DAPI. Finally, images were obtained using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) with an excitation wavelength of λ = 359 nm.

Various characterization experiments were performed to confirm the successful preparation of CDs, MnO2 NSs, and CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. UV–vis spectra were recorded for CDs, MnO2 NSs, and CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. The UV–vis spectrum of CDs solution showed a prominent absorption band at 359 nm (Fig. 1a, black line). MnO2 NSs exhibited a wide UV–vis absorption spectrum in the range of 250–500 nm (Fig. 1a, red line), consistent with the optical properties reported for MnO2 NSs (Peng et al., 2019; Sohal et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2018). Importantly, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 NSs significantly overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, resulting in efficient IFE between MnO2 NSs and CDs. Compared to bare MnO2 NSs, CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites exhibited a similar UV–vis spectrum but with a slight blue shift, possibly due to the adhesion of CDs to the surface of MnO2 NSs (Fig. 1a, blue line). Surface functional groups of these materials were characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). For CDs, distinct absorption peaks were observed at 3444 cm−1 (OH/NH stretching vibrations), 2979 cm−1 (CH) and 1650 cm−1 (CO). The band at 628 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of MnO2 NSs was attributed to MnO and MnOMn vibrations. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum of CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites encompassed typical peaks of both prepared CDs and MnO2, confirming the successful attachment of CDs to MnO2 NSs. The XRD test has also been performed to further investigate the crystal structure of CDs-MnO2, as observed in Fig. 1c, the peaks at 2θ degree of 31.49°, 45.39°, and 66.32° were correspond to the (104), (202), and (119) crystal planes of MnO2 (JCPDS 83-1763), respectively. While the typical broad peak at 2θ degree of 24.36° was associated with the graphitic structure of CDs. These results further confirmed the successful construction of CDs-MnO2. Moreover, the zeta potential of CDs and MnO2 NSs were − 13.7 mV and − 13.1 mV, respectively, indicating no electrostatic attraction between MnO2 NSs and CDs. CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites had a lower zeta potential (−28.53 mV), suggesting improved stability and dispersion with a shortened distance between MnO2 NSs and CDs (Fig. 1d).

XPS was employed for further characterization of CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites. The XPS spectra displayed peaks corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Mn 2p at 284.0 eV, 399.0 eV, 530.0 eV and 641.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e). In Fig. 1f, the three peaks at 284.0, 285.6 and 288.0 eV in the C1s spectrum belonged to the CC/CC, CO/CN and CO bonds, severally. The N 1 s high-resolution spectrum showed two obvious peaks at 398.9 and 399.5 eV corresponding to NH and CNC (Fig. 1g). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibited three peaks centered at 531.3, 532.6, and 536.8 eV, corresponding to MnOMn, MnOH, and HOH, respectively. The Mn 2p spectrum could be divided into two peaks at 641.5 and 653.1 eV, with a spin-energy separation of 11.6 eV, resembling the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons of MnO2 (Fig. 1i). Furthermore, the morphology of CDs, MnO2 NSs and CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites was observed using TEM. The results indicated that CDs had uniform size and nearly spherical shape with good dispersion (Fig. 1j). Both MnO2 NSs and CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites exhibited typical sheet-like structures and characteristic of a two-dimensional layered structure (Fig. 1k and Fig. 1l). Images revealed the presence of two-dimensional MnO2 NSs and clear adhesion of CDs to MnO2 NSs, confirming the successful attachment of CDs to MnO2 NSs. These results were consistent with literature reports (Garg et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022).

Initially, the optical stability of CDs-MnO2 was studied. As shown in the Fig. S1 and Table S1, after different time (0 h, 1 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h and 48 h) of exposure to UV light, the fluorescence of CDs-MnO2 itself showed almost no change, and the response intensity to GSH or GSH/ acrolein also remained unchanged. It demonstrates that our designed sensing platform is highly photostable. In addition, the thermal stability of CDs-MnO2 was investigated. As shown in Tables S2–3, after storage at different temperatures (−10 °C, 4 °C, 25 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C, 80 °C, and 100 °C) for 24 h, the fluorescence of CDs-MnO2 itself showed almost no change, and the response intensity to GSH or GSH/ acrolein also remained unchanged. The TG test results (Fig. S2) exhibited the weight loss of CDs-MnO2 was 4.753% before 250 °C, and decomposition begins at 250 °C and ends at 800 °C. The above experimental results demonstrate that CDs-MnO2 we prepared has good thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical response evaluation of CDs-MnO2 to GSH and acrolein
ce:section.ce:para: The optical response of CDs-MnO2 to GSH and acrolein was preliminarily assessed. As shown in Fig. 2a, upon the addition of GSH, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs-MnO2 exhibited a distinct blue shift compared to the original spectrum. Under sunlight, the color of the CDs-MnO2 solution changed from brown to colorless and transparent observed by the naked eye. However, when a mixture of acrolein and GSH was added to the CDs-MnO2 solution, the UV–vis spectrum also showed a different profile, with a noticeable blue shift. Under sunlight, the color of the CDs-MnO2 solution changed from colorless and transparent to reddish-brown (Fig. 2a, inset). Additionally, we investigated the fluorescence spectra of CDs-MnO2 for detecting GSH and acrolein. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 solution increased by approximately 200-fold upon GSH addition, and the fluorescence color of the CDs-MnO2 solution changed from non-fluorescent to blue under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2b, inset). However, when a mixture of acrolein and GSH was added, the fluorescence intensity and color of the CDs-MnO2 solution remained almost unchanged compared to the original. These results confirmed that GSH could effectively reduce MnO2, leading to the decomposition and recovery of CDs fluorescence, resulting in an “off-on” fluorescence response.

However, the specific Michael addition reaction between acrolein and GSH inhibited the reduction and decomposition of MnO2 NSs, causing the fluorescence signal of CDs-MnO2 to switch from “on” to “off.” Furthermore, the simultaneous color changes observed under sunlight and fluorescence confirmed that CDs-MnO2 could recognize and detect acrolein through both colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode approaches. Additionally, further research was conducted on the UV–vis spectra of CDs-MnO2 to different concentrations of GSH and acrolein. As shown in Fig. 2c, with increasing GSH concentration, the UV–vis spectra of CDs-MnO2 gradually changed, and the color of the CDs-MnO2 solution could be observed by the naked eye to change from brown to colorless under sunlight. Conversely, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2d, the color of the CDs-MnO2 solution could be observed by the naked eye to change from colorless to brown under sunlight gradually with increasing acrolein concentration.

Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the CDs-MnO2 to different concentrations of GSH and acrolein was investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2e, within the concentration range of 0–100.0 μM GSH, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2 at 442 nm gradually increased and showed a good linear relationship at the range of 0–30.0 μM (Fig. 2g). Under a 365 nm UV light, a visible change in fluorescence color from non-fluorescent to blue was observed with the increasing concentration of GSH in the CDs-MnO2 solution (Fig. 2e, inset). Before the measurement of the fluorescence response of the CDs-MnO2 to different concentrations of acrolein, GSH solution was mixed with different concentrations of acrolein for 10 min and then added to the CDs-MnO2 solution. As shown in Fig. 2f, within the range of 0.0–100.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 gradually decreased with the change in acrolein concentration. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2f, with the increase of acrolein concentration, the fluorescence color of the CDs-MnO2 solution changed from intense blue fluorescence to non-fluorescent under a 365 nm UV light. As revealed in Fig. 2h, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2 showed a good linear relationship with acrolein concentration with a linear range of 0–40.0 μM. The relationship equation was determined as y = −4637.89× + 184,282.94 (R2 = 0.9985), and the calculated detection limit was 0.067 μM. These experimental results demonstrate that CDs-MnO2 can achieve highly sensitive detection of acrolein with a low detection limit.

From the perspective of practical applications, this work also investigated the influence of response time and pH. Rapid detection is crucial for practical applications. As shown in Fig. S3a, the response time of the CDs-MnO2 to GSH and acrolein was studied. After adding GSH to the CDs-MnO2 solution, the fluorescence intensity rapidly increased and remained stable after 1 min. Conversely, when a mixture of acrolein and GSH was added, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 rapidly decreased and stabilized after 1 min (Fig. S3b). These results indicate a very rapid response time for the detection of acrolein using the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform.

The known pH values significantly affect fluorescence detection efficiency. Therefore, the influence of pH on fluorescence intensity was examined. As shown in Fig. S3c and Fig. S3d, within the pH range of 1.0–12.0, after adding GSH or GSH/acrolein, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 initially increased, then reached a peak and gradually decreased. Optimal fluorescence intensity and stability were observed at pH 6.0–8.0, indicating the suitability of this method for detecting GSH and acrolein under physiological conditions, such as in live cells. Beyond the pH range of 6.0–8.0, the addition of GSH or acrolein led to a decrease in fluorescence intensity, suggesting fluorescence instability of CDs-MnO2 under strong acidic or alkaline conditions. Therefore, this study selected pH 6.0–8.0 as the appropriate pH conditions for the recognition and detection of GSH and acrolein by CDs-MnO2.

Good selectivity is a crucial criterion for evaluating the quality of a probe. Therefore, the selectivity of the CDs-MnO2 was studied. Firstly, a variety of analytes, including GSH, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, PO3 2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, glucose, glutamate, glycine, histidine, proline, and leucine, were tested using the CDs-MnO2. As shown in Fig. S4a, only the addition of GSH caused a noticeable change in the UV–vis spectrum profile of CDs-MnO2, while the presence of other analytes had minimal impact on the UV–vis spectrum of CDs-MnO2. Moreover, under sunlight, only the addition of GSH resulted in the color change of the CDs-MnO2 solution from brown to colorless and transparent (Fig. S4a, inset). A series of analytes were also investigated for their impact on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs-MnO2. As can be seen in Fig. S4c, only GSH caused a significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2, while other analytes had no obvious effect. Additionally, under a 365 nm UV light, visible to the naked eye, only GSH could restore the blue fluorescence of CDs-MnO2 (Fig. S4c, inset).

Similarly, to verify the specificity of CDs-MnO2 for detecting acrolein, a series of analytes including acrolein, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, glyoxal, methylglyoxal, acetone, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, PO3 2−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NH3·H2O, and N2H4·H2O were mixed with GSH, and the resulting mixtures were separately added to the CDs-MnO2 solution to determine their UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. S4b, only acrolein caused a significant redshift in the UV–vis spectrum of CDs-MnO2, and only the mixture of acrolein and GSH changed the solution color to yellowish-brown under sunlight. In Fig. S4d, when other analytes were added in combination with GSH, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 remained high. Only the mixture of acrolein and GSH caused a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2. Under a 365 nm UV light, visible to the naked eye, only the mixture of acrolein and GSH could induce an “on-off” fluorescence response (Fig. S4d, inset). In addition, the interference experiments of various mental ions (Fig. S5) further indicate that under the mediation of GSH, the CDs-MnO2 can realize the acrolein detection with high selectivity and strong anti-interference performance.

This study also investigated the mechanism of CDs-MnO2 detecting acrolein. Fig. S6 showed the fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-MnO2 in the presence of GSH, acrolein, and GSH/acrolein respectively. It can be observed that GSH, acrolein, and GSH/acrolein had almost no impact on the fluorescence intensity of CDs compared to when CDs are present alone. However, when CDs formed a nanocomposite with MnO2 NSs to generate CDs-MnO2, the fluorescence was completely quenched, indicating that MnO2 NSs quenched the fluorescence of CDs. As mentioned earlier, MnO2 exhibited a broader absorption band in the range of 250–500 nm, significantly overlapping with the spectrum of CDs (300–400 nm) (Fig. 1a). The overlap in the UV–vis spectrum further supported that the most likely sensing mechanism between CDs and MnO2 NSs was the IFE. Upon the addition of GSH, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-MnO2 significantly increased, indicating that GSH reduced MnO2 NSs, decomposing them into Mn2+ ions (Eq. 1), and restoring the fluorescence of CDs (Scheme 1b). However, as shown in Fig. S6, when only acrolein or GSH/acrolein was added, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MnO2 changed little compared to CDs-MnO2 alone. This suggested that acrolein itself cannot interact with CDs-MnO2, but it can undergo a specific nucleophilic addition reaction with GSH (Eq. 2), inhibiting the reduction and decomposition of MnO2 NSs by GSH, thereby blocking the release of CDs and fluorescence recovery. These experiments demonstrate the recognition mechanism of CDs-MnO2 for acrolein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of GSH and acrolein by CDs-MnO2 hydrogel combined with smartphone detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, portable sensor-based point-of-care detection technology has continuously emerged and developed. To obtain instant, portable, and accurate detection results, smartphones with powerful image processing capabilities have gradually been applied in the field of point-of-care detection. Therefore, we prepared CDs-MnO2 hydrogel and combined it with a smartphone to establish a smart detection platform, further achieving point-of-care detection of acrolein in actual samples. Fig. 3a illustrated the construction of CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films. As shown in Fig. 3b, the hydrogel films we prepared were smooth, intact, flexible, and resistant to breakage even when bent arbitrarily. CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films appeared yellow-brown under daylight and showed no fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light. When different concentrations of GSH solutions were dropped onto CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films, the yellow-brown hydrogel gradually turned colorless and transparent, with the blue fluorescence gradually recovering. However, when a mixture of GSH and acrolein was dropped onto CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films, maintaining a constant GSH concentration and increasing acrolein concentration, the hydrogel films changed from colorless and transparent to yellow-brown under daylight, and the fluorescence gradually changed from intense blue fluorescence to weak fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3b). This indicated that the binding of GSH to acrolein inhibits the fluorescence recovery of CDs by GSH, causing the hydrogel film to gradually return to its initial color and fluorescence state with the increase of acrolein concentration. This change can be used to detect and quantify acrolein. By combining the hydrogel film with a smartphone, we further enhance the sensitivity and intelligence of acrolein detection in practical applications. Firstly, images of the hydrogel film detecting acrolein were captured using a smartphone, then uploaded to an APP, and converted into RGB values. With the increase of/in acrolein concentration, the blue fluorescence of CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films was gradually quenched, and the decrease in blue fluorescence was accompanied by a decrease in the R + G value. As shown in Fig. 3c, within the concentration range of 0–20.0 μM, the R + G value was well linearly correlated with the concentration of acrolein, with the equation y = −15.3007× + 320.6813 (R2 = 0.9952), and the calculated detection limit was 0.058 μM. These results indicate that the portable smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing platform has great potential for point-of-care detection of acrolein.

The intra-day accuracy and precision for this method were 97.5–104.3% and 2.4–3.2%, respectively, while the inter-day accuracy varied between 98.9% and 102.6% and the precision ranged from 2.0% to 3.4% (Table S4). The results of the intra- and inter-assay precisions and accuracies indicate that this method was suitable and reliable for acrolein analysis. To further validate the applicability of this smart detection platform, five different brands of mobile phones were selected as smartphones for acrolein analysis. As can be seen from Tables S5–7, R + G values obtained from the five smartphones were identical with the RSD ranging from 0.3 to 4.0%, demonstrating strong applicability of this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein, and on-site evaluation of acrolein scavengers' scavenging capacity
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we investigated the UV–vis absorption spectra of acrolein with different concentrations of GSH. As shown in Fig. 4a, the maximum UV–vis absorption peak of acrolein itself was at 315 nm. As the concentration of GSH increased, the absorbance of acrolein at 315 nm gradually decreased until it disappeared, indicating that acrolein was gradually consumed by GSH. We found that there was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9953) between the absorbance of acrolein at 315 nm and the concentration of GSH (Fig. 4b). According to this linear relationship, we can determine the concentration of GSH by measuring the absorbance of the sample and then calculate the amount of removed acrolein. To further validate the integrated ability of the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform to detect and remove acrolein, we simultaneously used the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform method and the UV spectrophotometry method to detect and remove a certain amount of acrolein, and results of above two methods were compared. As shown in Fig. 4c, after GSH removed acrolein, the free acrolein was quickly determined using CDs-MnO2 hydrogel, which was almost consistent with the results obtained using the UV spectrophotometry method. These results indicate that the CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform can achieve the integration of acrolein detection and removal with high accuracy and sensitivity.

To further expand the potential applications of this nanoplatform, we selected three literature-reported effective acrolein scavengers to be incubated with acrolein. We utilized CDs-MnO2 hydrogel to measure the residual free acrolein after removal, aiming to validate its on-site evaluation capability for the removal efficacy of acrolein scavengers. As shown in Fig. 4d-f, the free acrolein without scavengers was significantly high, while after the addition of scavengers, the free acrolein gradually decreased over time. When the incubation time was 60 min, the content of free acrolein reached its lowest level and stabilized. Consistent with previous literature validation results (Jiang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2022), these three scavengers demonstrated strong acrolein removal capabilities. This also indicated that CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform can accurately, conveniently, and rapidly evaluate the scavenging capacity of different acrolein scavengers, eliminating the need for complex sample pretreatment processes and bulky instrument usage. Therefore, the method described in this paper provides a novel approach for the screening of acrolein scavengers and on-site evaluation of their removal efficacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel combined with smartphone detection platform
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility and explore the versatility of the smart fluorescence hydrogel sensor, we selected some actual samples such as baijiu, deep-fried meat skewers, and burning cigarettes for detection using hydrogel films. As shown in Fig. 5a, first, the sample was placed in a culture dish with a cup of GSH solution. Then, the culture dish was sealed with a glass cover to avoid light and placed at room temperature for a certain period. After some time, the glass cover was opened, and the GSH solution in the culture dish was dropped onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film using a pipette. After 15 min, changes in the color and fluorescence of the hydrogel film were observed with the naked eye under both daylight and UV light. Simultaneously, photos of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film were taken using a smartphone.

It can be seen From Fig. 5b and Fig. 5c, when the GSH solution was dropped onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film, the film was colorless and transparent under daylight and showed intense blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light at 0 h. This indicated that at this time, due to the short placement time, the food sample had not released acrolein, and the GSH solution had not reacted with acrolein. After a certain period, when GSH solution was dropped onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film, with the prolonged placement time, it can be observed with the naked eye that the blue fluorescence of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film gradually weakened. When placed for 24 h, dropping GSH solution onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film resulted in the film appearing yellow-brown under daylight, with no fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light. This indicated that after a long period of placement, the GSH solution had absorbed acrolein released from the food sample and reacted with it, inhibiting the fluorescence recovery of the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film. Similarly, we used the same method to examine the detection of acrolein released from a burning cigarette (Fig. 5d). When the cigarette burned out, GSH solution was dropped onto the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel film. The hydrogel film appeared yellow-brown under daylight, with no fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. This indicated that after burning the cigarette, the air contained a large amount of acrolein, which reacted with the GSH solution. The above experimental results show that the prepared CDs-MnO2 hydrogel films can effectively and instantly detect changes in the acrolein content of food and ambient air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of acrolein in real food samples and ambient air of daily living environments
ce:section.ce:para: The study revealed that acrolein was widely present in various foods, including fried and baked products, overheated vegetable oils and animal fats, alcoholic beverages, fruits and vegetables (Feng et al., 2006; Stevens & Maier, 2008; Zou et al., 2021). To further evaluate the application capability of CDs-MnO2 hydrogels in various real food samples, we chose potato chips, cheese, and apple juice as the subjects of our study. Additionally, considering that acrolein was widely present in the ambient air of everyday living environments, we selected the kitchen, smoking room, and barbecue restaurant as places for point-of-care acrolein detection. As shown in Table 2 , the results were consistent with the actual added concentrations of acrolein in the samples. The method showed recovery rates ranged from 97.01% to 104.65%, and RSD values were no more than 4.16%, demonstrating good reliability and repeatability. The results indicate that CDs-MnO2/GSH nanoplatform can be used for the determination of acrolein in various real samples, and the CDs-MnO2 hydrogel method provides high accuracy and reliability for the quantitative determination of acrolein in food samples and ambient air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of acrolein in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The above experiments preliminarily demonstrated the ability of CDs-MnO2 to detect acrolein in complex systems. We further investigated the detection capability of CDs-MnO2 in living cells. Firstly, the standard MTT assay was employed to evaluate the toxicity of CDs-MnO2 to GL261 cells. As shown in Fig. S7, after treatment with different concentrations of CDs-MnO2 for 24 h, cell viability was above 95%, indicating that CDs-MnO2 had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility in living cells. As shown in Fig. 6 , faint blue fluorescence was observed in the blue channel only after incubating with CDs-MnO2 (10.0 μM) for 12 h (Fig. 6a), indicating a low GSH concentration in GL261 cells. To increase the GSH concentration in the cells, LA (100.0 μM) was applied to the culture dish for 2 h, followed by the addition of CDs-MnO2 for another 12 h. It was observed that the blue fluorescence in the blue channel was significantly enhanced compared to using CDs-MnO2 alone (Fig. 6b). To further validate the detection capability of the CDs-MnO2 system for acrolein in GL261 cells, LA-treated GL261 cells were incubated with acrolein for 2 h, followed by the addition of CDs-MnO2 for 12 h. As seen from Fig. 6c, the fluorescence in the blue channel was almost completely quenched. These results indicate that CDs-MnO2 can effectively detect acrolein in living cells, providing a reliable and powerful tool for the detection and visualization of acrolein in living cells.

In this study, we have developed a novel fluorescent nanoplatform based on CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites for the simultaneous detection and removal of acrolein. Within CDs-MnO2 nanocomposites, MnO2 NSs quenched the fluorescence of CDs through IFE. Upon the introduction of GSH, Mn4+ in MnO2 NSs was reduced to free Mn2+, leading to the decomposition of MnO2 NSs and release of CDs, subsequently resulting in complete fluorescence recovery and generating an “off-on” fluorescence response. In the presence of acrolein, GSH selectively removed acrolein, while the consumption of GSH inhibited the GSH-induced decomposition of MnO2 NSs, suppressing the fluorescence recovery of CDs and generating an “on-off” fluorescence response. Therefore, under the mediation of GSH, the CDs-MnO2 enabled integrated sensitive detection and effective removal of acrolein. The detection limit (LOD) was 0.067 μM. Furthermore, to enhance practical portability, we combined CDs-MnO2 hydrogel with smartphones for point-of-care detection of acrolein in food samples and ambient air (LOD was 0.058 μM), yielding satisfactory results (recovery rates varied between 97.01–104.65%, and RSD ranged from 1.42 to 4.16%). Additionally, the capability of this nanoplatform for on-site evaluation of the efficacy of acrolein scavengers was demonstrated. Moreover, the nanoplatform had been successfully utilized for the detection of acrolein in biological cells, providing a valuable and promising tool for the diagnosis and treatment of acrolein-induced diseases. This study presents a novel strategy for all-in-one sensing and removal of acrolein, with broad prospects for applications in food, environment, and biomedical fields.
